steps.text: Include a description of how custom-prepared materials were prepared and documentation of their homogeneity in the report.
Provide a summary of the study design, procedures, and statistical methods used for analyses of the results.
Present and discuss the numerical and descriptive specifications for the performance attributes of the methods.
Develop quantitative QC criteria for initial and ongoing method/laboratory performance assessments.
Provide a statistical assessment of the method's comparability with any available reference method.

preconditions.text: If any materials were custom-prepared for the study

steps.text: List identification criteria used.
Provide equations used to derive final sample results from typical instrument data.
Provide discussion of estimating detection limits.
Incorporate a precision/bias statement in Section 13.0, including detection limits and source/limitations of data.
Provide detailed description of method performance, including data on precision, bias, detection limits, and statistical procedures used.
Provide a side-by-side comparison of performance versus reference method specifications.
Describe aspects of this method that minimize reagents or prevent pollution in Section 14.0.
Cite how waste and samples are minimized and properly disposed in Section 15.0.

preconditions.text: Performance is tested relative to the reference method.

steps.text: Conduct a readiness review of each participating laboratory to ensure compliance with EPA's Quality Assurance Project Plan 2003-01.
Provide the method protocols, standardized test forms, data sheets, media preparation sheets, selected media and reagents, test chemicals, and inoculated porcelain penicylinders to each laboratory.
Provide test parameters for each chemical treatment describing the conditions for testing to each laboratory.
Prepare the actual test formulations (i.e., perform dilutions) on-site.
Randomize the test scheme to account for potential subjectivity by the analysts.

constraints.text: Instability of test chemicals such as diluted sodium hypochlorite

steps.text: Produce spores for use in the TSM per the method provided.
Inoculate carriers from one spore preparation per laboratory, ensuring enough carriers are inoculated to perform the entire study (approximately 150).

constraints.text: The mean target carrier load for the TSM must be 1.0 × 10^7 spores/carrier or 7.0 logs per carrier.
No organic burden will be added to the spore inoculum.

steps.text: Use SOPs and standard forms for routine operations, including test methodology, analytical procedures, and calibration procedures.
Maintain and archive selected electronic spreadsheets and email as official documentation.
Track all preparations of test chemicals, media, and reagents using an assigned media preparation number.
Maintain samples and test chemicals to ensure their integrity.
Store test chemicals away from standards, media, and reagents.
Maintain and archive specific information on source, identification, and volume received for all test chemicals.
Inspect all critical supplies and materials (such as media, reagents, carriers, and test chemicals) prior to use to ensure the shipment has not been damaged or compromised.
Perform performance testing (sterility and suitability to support growth of B. subtilis) on growth media a minimum of one time, preferably on the first batch prepared per lot.

preconditions.text: If using SOPs comparable to EPA's, obtain concurrence by the Study Director or the EPA Quality Assurance Unit.

constraints.text: For pre-sterilized lab supplies, the manufacturer’s statement of sterility is acceptable for quality control documentation; no further testing is required.

steps.text: Initiate B. subtilis culture (e.g., use nutrient broth to rehydrate a lyophilized culture, and incubate the broth culture for 24 ± 2 hours at 36 ± 1ºC prior to streak inoculation).
Streak inoculate a set (e.g., six) nutrient agar slopes and incubate 24 ± 2 hours at 36 ± 1ºC.
Perform purity and identification confirmation testing for QC (e.g., colony morphology on TSA, Gram stain, or use of other identification systems).
Following incubation, store at 2-5ºC.
Maintain stock culture on nutrient agar slants by monthly (30 ± 2 days) transfers.

Using growth from a stock culture tube, inoculate 10 mL tubes (e.g., 2 tubes, depending on the amount of spore preparation desired) of nutrient broth and incubate tubes 24 ± 2 hr on an orbital shaker at approximately 150 rpm at 36 ± 1ºC.
Use this culture to inoculate amended nutrient agar plates.
Inoculate each plate with 500 µl of broth culture and spread the inoculum with a sterile bent gl

preconditions.text: Broth culture has been incubated for 24 ± 2 hours at 36 ± 1ºC

steps.text: Define the intended use of the method, manner of data analysis, and the environmental matrix or matrices of interest.
Evaluate the criteria specified in Section 2.2 of the main report, taking into account accuracy, precision, relative cost, and level of training required.

preconditions.text: A particular PCR method is being considered for validation.

constraints.text: PCR methods generally will be unable to distinguish viable organisms unless modifications like reverse transcription PCR or pretreatment of samples with intercalating dyes are used.

steps.text: Design controls to estimate sample preparation efficiency as well as PCR assay inhibition.
Add an amplification control sequence to each reaction to distinguish between false and true negatives.

constraints.text: PCR inhibitors can completely prevent the amplification of target nucleic acids or reduce sensitivity resulting in false negatives or incorrect real-time quantitative PCR measurements.

steps.text: Perform mixture studies to measure the ability of the PCR method to obtain reliable results from mixed source samples.
Determine the amount of variability observed from a series of repeated measurements of a reference standard.
Calculate the relative standard deviation (RSD) using the formula: RSD = (standard deviation of measurements x 100) / mean.

preconditions.text: The PCR method is designed to detect target sequences in complex mixtures of heterologous nucleic acid preparations that can significantly vary in composition and amount from one sample to the next.

constraints.text: Mixture ratios should represent the range of conditions that may be encountered when implementing the method (SWGDAM 2004).

steps.text: Measure accuracy with blind spikes of DNA targets and blind spikes of the whole organism.
Spike known DNA targets into molecular grade water and a characterized matrix with known quantities of potential chemical and microbial interferences as well as inert substances.
Split samples of uncharacterized matrices containing blind spike material for analysis in different laboratories.

constraints.text: Careful preparation and handling of standards for accuracy measurements is critical for the estimation of PCR method accuracy.

steps.text: Experimentally assess the equivalence of method performance with all systems being considered.
Perform preliminary assessments of specificity through the analysis of common DNA standards from one or more non-target controls that are closely related to the target of interest.
Adjust the annealing temperature of the thermal cycling protocol for each respective reagent and instrument to bring it within acceptable levels.
Predetermine the sensitivity of an assay on different instruments by the analysis of common DNA standards.
Perform instrument-specific adjustments in thermal cycling parameters or exclude less sensitive instruments from being utilized in a validation study with that reagent.

preconditions.text: Prior to performing a collaborative validation study involving multiple instrument and/or reagent systems
If variability in specificity is observed
If large differences in sensitivity with a given reagent are observed

constraints.text: Particular attention should be paid to any significant differences in specificity and sensitivity with these systems.

steps.text: Define sensitivity and LOQ characteristics beforehand for each platform.
Take the differences in sensitivity and LOQ into consideration in the design of a validation study.
Specify the chemistry and commercial source of reagents used for method development for each real-time PCR method.
Experimentally demonstrate that alternative reagent products provide equivalent performance to the originally specified source.
Designate alternative sources of reagents.

preconditions.text: Acceptable minor differences in sensitivity and LOQ are observed, and it is economically feasible.

steps.text: Ensure through laboratory testing that products are efficacious.
Maintain the testing data on file.
Submit efficacy data to support each label claim and use pattern.

preconditions.text: If registering non-public health antimicrobial products
If registering public health antimicrobial products

Prior to pursuing method validation, knowledge of the target performance standard is essential (e.g., if a 6-log10 kill of the target organism on a porcelain carrier is required for sporicidal decontamination products, then the efficacy method must be able to accommodate a minimum of a 7-log10 challenge per carrier).

constraints.text: For carrier-based tests, the method must include an inoculation procedure, a method of determining populations of microorganisms on a carrier, and a target range for the microorganism population.
The method must include a neutralization confirmation procedure.
The method must address means for managing contact time and temperatures (i.e., includes references to calibrated timers and thermometers).
